perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of the Commonwealth of Virginia and all actions involving this Agreement shall be brought in the state or federal courts located in the Commonwealth of Virginia, to which the parties consent to jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of Virginia.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause that requires all actions involving the agreement to be brought in Virginia courts and that the parties consent to jurisdiction, the agreement now contradicts Virginia law regarding mandatory venue provisions in certain consumer contracts.
perturbation.law_explanation: Virginia Code § 8.01-26 outlines venue requirements, including the ability for parties to agree to venue, but also provides certain protections for consumers. By removing the venue consent, and depending on the nature of the 'Receiver' and the subject matter, it could be argued that the agreement attempts to circumvent mandatory venue provisions designed to protect Virginia residents. This creates a legal risk if the Receiver is a consumer. The original text correctly established venue in Virginia.

Once the report is generated you'll then have the option to download it as a pdf, print or email the report. Code of Virginia Title 8.01. Civil Remedies and Procedure Chapter 3. Actions 6/23/2025 § 8.01-26 . Assignment of causes of action. Only those causes of action for damage to real or personal property, whether such damage be direct or indirect, and causes of action ex contractu are assignable. The provisions of this section shall not prohibit any injured party or his estate from making a voluntary assignment of the proceeds or anticipated proceeds of any court award or settlement as security for new value given in consideration of such voluntary assignment. 1977, c. 617; 1991, c. 256. The chapters of the acts of assembly referenced in the historical citation at the end of this section may not constitute a comprehensive list of such chapters and may exclude chapters whose provisions have expired. Virginia Law Library The Code of Virginia, Constitution of Virginia, Charters, Authorities, Compacts and Uncodified Acts are now available in EPub eBook format. Helpful Resources Virginia Code Commission Virginia Register of Regulations U.S. Constitution For Developers The Virginia Law website data is available via a web service.
perturbation.justification: The original text ensured that any legal actions related to the agreement would take place in Virginia courts, with the parties consenting to that jurisdiction. The changed text removes this explicit agreement to venue and jurisdiction within Virginia. According to the Perturbation Explanation, this omission could potentially contradict Virginia law regarding mandatory venue provisions, especially in consumer contracts. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 focuses on the assignability of causes of action and does not directly address venue, the Law Explanation suggests that removing the venue consent could circumvent protections for Virginia residents under Virginia Code § 8.01-26, depending on the nature of the agreement and parties involved. The absence of a clause ensuring venue in Virginia weakens consumer protection and creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Receiver shall use the same degree of care to protect and safeguard the confidentiality of Confidential Information as it uses to protect and safeguard the confidentiality of its own proprietary info rmation, and Receiver represents, warrants and covenants to VIDAR that such degree of care is reasonably designed to protect the confidentiality of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiver shall use a reasonable degree of care to protect and safeguard the confidentiality of Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause requires Receiver to use the *same degree of care* as they use for their own proprietary information, and warrants that this degree of care is *reasonably designed* to protect the information. The removal of the warranty creates a legal loophole by not requiring the receiver to warrant that their own proprietary confidentiality measures are adequate. It becomes a simple reasonable standard, which is a very low bar.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets to qualify for protection. While 'reasonable degree of care' might seem compliant, removing the warranty that the receiver's care is 'reasonably designed' to protect confidentiality, and making it just a reasonable standard, weakens VIDAR's position under VUTSA if VIDAR needs to seek an injunction to protect its trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 59.1-336. Once the report is generated you'll then have the option to download it as a pdf, print or email the report. Code of Virginia Title 59.1. Trade and Commerce Chapter 26. Uniform Trade Secrets Act 6/23/2025 § 59.1-336 . Short title and definitions. As used in this chapter, which may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, unless the context requires otherwise: "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, use of a computer or computer network without authority, breach of a duty or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. "Misappropriation" means: 1. Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or 2. Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who a. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or b. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was (1) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (2) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; (3) Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (4) Acquired by accident or mistake. "Person" means a natural pe
perturbation.justification: The original text provided a warranty that the receiver's care in protecting confidential information was 'reasonably designed' for that purpose. The modified text removes this warranty, only requiring a 'reasonable degree of care'. According to the LLM law explanation, which references the Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act (VUTSA), this weakens VIDAR's position if it needs to seek an injunction to protect its trade secrets, as it lowers the standard of care required. While snippet 1 defines terms like 'improper means' and 'misappropriation' within the VUTSA, it doesn't directly address the standard of care required to maintain secrecy. However, the LLM explanation coupled with the removal of the warranty clearly creates a legal loophole. The scraped snippet not directly mentioning 'reasonable degree of care' does not negate the validity of the contradiction since other components point to a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Receiver shall promptly disclose a ll Intellectual Property to VIDAR and shall make and maintain adequate and current written records of all Intellectual Property.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiver shall disclose Intellectual Property to VIDAR.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement to 'maintain adequate and current written records of all Intellectual Property,' the agreement weakens VIDAR's ability to track and enforce its intellectual property rights. This creates a contradiction with general principles of intellectual property law that favor clear documentation and record-keeping for establishing ownership and priority.
perturbation.law_explanation: 17 U.S. Code § 205 deals with the recordation of transfers of copyright ownership. While this NDA doesn't explicitly transfer ownership (it assigns future IP), the absence of a clear requirement for the Receiver to maintain records hinders VIDAR's ability to later record transfers and establish clear ownership, especially for derivative works or improvements created by the Receiver. Without the receiver making adequate current written records, it is hard for VIDAR to know of, and record, all intellectual property.

A sworn or official certification may be s
perturbation.justification: The original contract requires the Receiver to maintain "adequate and current written records" of all Intellectual Property. The changed contract removes this requirement. The Law Explanation describes how the absence of this requirement hinders VIDAR's ability to record transfers and establish clear ownership, especially for derivative works or improvements created by the Receiver. 17 U.S. Code § 205, as provided in the scraped snippet 2, deals with the recordation of transfers of copyright ownership. By removing the requirement to maintain records, the modified text weakens VIDAR's ability to comply with 17 U.S. Code § 205, because it is harder to know of, and record, all intellectual property.

perturbation.original_text: All Intellectual Property that are origi nal works of authorship shall be considered works made for hire (as that phrase is used in Section 101 of the United States Copyright Act, 17 U.S.C. § 101 or analogous la w). If any Intellectual Property is found not to be a work made for hire and/or is owned by Receiver or any third party, Receiver hereby irrevocably (and shall cause any third party owner to) assigns and transfers all rights to and inte rests in such Intellectual Property to VIDAR without further consideration.
perturbation.changed_text: All Intellectual Property that are origi nal works of authorship shall be considered works made for hire (as that phrase is used in Section 101 of the United States Copyright Act, 17 U.S.C. § 101 or analogous la w).
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause that mandates assignment of intellectual property rights to VIDAR if the 'work for hire' provision is deemed inapplicable, the contract now contradicts standard practices for intellectual property protection and assignment. This creates a legal risk for VIDAR, as it may not automatically own all the intellectual property developed by the Receiver.
perturbation.law_explanation: 17 U.S. Code § 204 requires transfers of copyright ownership to be in writing and signed by the owner. By removing the assignment clause, if a court finds that the 'work for hire' provision doesn't apply, VIDAR will not automatically own the copyright to the Receiver's work. VIDAR would then need a separate written assignment to obtain ownership, and the Receiver may refuse, or demand additional compensation, because there is no agreement in place forcing them to assign their rights. VIDAR will only own IP classified as 'works for hire', which may not be everything. Without the clause, VIDAR will have trouble enforcing its rights over anything else, since it needs a signed agreement.

(b) A certificate of acknowledgement is not required for the validity of a transfer, but is prima facie evidence of the execution of the transfer if- (1) in the case of a
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes a clause that ensured VIDAR's ownership of intellectual property developed by the Receiver, specifically if the 'work for hire' provision doesn't apply. This contradicts 17 U.S. Code § 204, which requires transfers of copyright ownership to be in writing and signed by the owner. Without the assignment clause, VIDAR would need a separate written agreement to obtain ownership of the Receiver's work, which the Receiver may refuse. The scraped law snippet explicitly states: "(a) A transfer of copyright ownership, other than by operation of law, is not valid unless an instrument of conveyance, or a note or memorandum of the transfer, is in writing and signed by the owner of the rights conveyed or such owner's duly authorized agent." By removing the clause that would have acted as an instrument of conveyance, the contract now violates this requirement if the work is not considered 'work for hire'.
